title: Draughtproofing a front door, letterbox and keyhole
description: How to draughtproof a front door, letterbox and keyhole for under £40, with UK costs, payback figures and the fitting mistakes that stop seals working.
blurb: Seal the perimeter, letterbox, keyhole and threshold for under £40 and stop the hallway stealing heat all winter.
lede: Stand in your hallway on a windy January evening and you can usually feel exactly where your heating money is going. A typical front door has four separate leaks: the gap around the edge of the door, the letterbox, the keyhole, and the gap under the threshold. None of them looks like much on its own, but together they amount to a permanent open window a few centimetres square, positioned on the most wind-blasted face of the house. The good news is that this is one of the cheapest fixes in home energy. Every part you need is a standard DIY item, the whole job takes about an hour, and the total bill is usually £20 to £40.
short_answer: A full front door draughtproofing kit costs roughly £20 to £40: self-adhesive rubber strip for the frame, a brush plate for the letterbox, a covered escutcheon for the keyhole and a brush or seal for the threshold. The Energy Saving Trust puts draught-proofing windows and doors at around £30 a year for a typical gas-heated semi, and a leaky front door is usually the single worst offender, so the kit normally pays for itself within one to two winters. The comfort improvement in the hallway is immediate.

sections.h2: Why the front door is usually the leakiest door in the house
sections.html:
Internal doors only ever move air between rooms at the same temperature, so a gap under the lounge door costs you comfort but not much money. The front door is different. It separates a heated hallway from outside air, it sits on the face of the house that takes the most wind, and it is the only door in the building with deliberate holes cut through it for post and a key.

Wind pressure matters more than people expect. A draught is not just warm air gently wandering out; on an exposed door, cold air is actively pushed in through every gap, and the warm air it displaces leaves through gaps higher up the house. That stack effect is why a leaky front door makes upstairs feel colder too. It is also why the hallway in many British houses is the room nobody can heat: the radiator there spends its life warming a stream of outside air that immediately leaves again.

The Energy Saving Trust estimates that draught-proofing around windows and doors saves around £30 a year in a typical gas-heated semi-detached home, and that full draught-proofing of windows, doors and floors saves around £85 a year. Within that, the front door usually carries an outsized share, because it combines the biggest perimeter gap in the house with two purpose-made holes. If your heating struggles to get the hallway above 16C, this is almost always why.

sections.h2: Find the gaps before you buy anything
sections.html:
Five minutes of testing tells you which parts you actually need, and it is much easier to do on a cold, breezy day.

  • The back-of-hand test. Run the back of your hand slowly around the closed door, along the hinge side, the latch side, the top and the bottom. The back of your hand is far more sensitive to moving air than your palm. Note where you feel cold air, because draughts are rarely even all the way round.
  • The smoke test. An incense stick or a just-blown-out candle held near the edges shows even faint air movement. Watch the smoke get dragged towards or away from the gap.
  • The daylight test. Turn the hallway light off in daytime and look around the closed door from inside. Any sliver of daylight is a gap big enough to matter, and a bright line under the door means the threshold is your first job.
  • The paper test. Close the door on a strip of paper. If you can pull it out without resistance at any point, the door is not actually contacting its frame there, with or without an old seal.
  • Letterbox and keyhole. Push the letterbox flap gently from inside; if it swings freely or rattles in wind, it is leaking constantly. A keyhole that whistles in a gale needs no further diagnosis.

While you are at it, check the obvious neighbours: an open chimney in the hallway or front room loses far more than the door itself, and a chimney draught excluder is the same kind of cheap, fast fix.

sections.h2: Fix each gap, part by part
sections.html:
The perimeter: seal the frame, not the door

For the gap around the top and sides, the standard fix is self-adhesive strip stuck to the door frame's stop, so the door compresses it as it closes. Skip the cheapest foam tape, which flattens permanently within a season. EPDM rubber strip in a P or E profile costs £5 to £10 a roll, lasts years and seals gaps up to about 5mm. For wonky older doors with uneven gaps, screw-fixed brush strip or V-shaped seals cope better with variation. Two fitting rules do most of the work: clean the frame properly first so the adhesive actually holds, and match the profile size to your measured gap. A strip that is too thick will stop the door latching, and a door slammed against an oversized seal soon tears it off.

The letterbox: a brush plate on the inside face

The letterbox is a hole through the door that the original flap barely covers. The fix is an internal letterbox brush plate, £5 to £12, with two rows of brush and ideally a hinged flap as well. It screws over the slot on the inside face of the door in about ten minutes. Check that post still passes through reasonably freely, or you will be picking shredded envelopes off the mat. If draughts are severe and you get little post, an external cowl over the flap adds a second line of defence, and the nuclear option is sealing the slot entirely and fitting a wall-mounted post box.

The keyhole: a covered escutcheon

A traditional mortice keyhole is a direct air path through the full thickness of the door. A covered escutcheon, which is a keyhole plate with a small swinging disc over the opening, costs £2 to £5 and fits with two screws. You flick the cover aside with the key, so it costs nothing in daily convenience. Modern uPVC and composite doors with euro cylinders rarely need this, because the cylinder itself fills the hole.

The threshold: brush strip or proper door bottom seal

The gap under the door is usually the biggest single leak. A screw-on brush strip for the inside face of the door costs £8 to £15 and suits uneven floors. A flexible blade seal gives a tighter result on a flat threshold. For large gaps, fit a threshold bar with a rubber insert on the floor and a matching seal on the door. The classic fabric sausage costs nothing and works while it is in place, but it falls over every time the door opens, so treat it as a stopgap rather than the fix.

While you are there

An old cat flap with a warped or missing magnet leaks like a second letterbox; replace the flap or the whole unit. And if the glazing panel around the door rattles, a bead of frame sealant closes that path too.

sections.h2: What it costs and what it saves
sections.html:
Here is the realistic shopping list for a complete front door job, with typical DIY store prices.

FixTypical DIY costTime to fitWhat it deals with
EPDM rubber strip around the frame£5 to £1020 minutesPerimeter gap on top and sides
Letterbox brush plate with flap£5 to £1210 minutesThe letterbox slot
Covered escutcheon£2 to £55 minutesThe keyhole
Door bottom brush or blade seal£8 to £1520 minutesThe threshold gap
Whole door£20 to £42About an hourAll four leaks

Typical DIY prices, checked June 2026. Savings context: Energy Saving Trust estimates around £30 a year for draught-proofing windows and doors, and around £85 a year for full draught-proofing, in a typical gas-heated semi-detached home, with gas valued at 7.33p per kWh under the Ofgem cap for July to September 2026.

On those numbers, a £25 to £40 spend against £20 to £30 a year of avoided gas pays back within one to two winters, and the parts last for years. There is also a second-order saving that is harder to put a precise number on but very real: a draught-free hallway feels warmer at the same air temperature, because moving air strips heat from your skin. The Energy Saving Trust notes that turning a thermostat down from 22C to 21C saves about £90 a year in Great Britain, and removing the cold blast at the front door is exactly the kind of change that lets people nudge the dial down without noticing.

sections.h2: Do not seal yourself into a condensation problem
sections.html:
Draughtproofing removes uncontrolled leaks, and that is all it should remove. Houses still need deliberate ventilation to clear moisture from cooking, showers and breathing, and a handful of openings must stay clear:

  • Trickle vents in window frames and any wall vents in habitable rooms.
  • Permanent air vents serving an open-flued boiler, gas fire or solid fuel stove. Never block these; the appliance needs that air to burn safely.
  • Extractor fans in kitchens and bathrooms, and underfloor airbricks below suspended timber floors.

The distinction is simple: a draught is air leaking through a gap that was never designed to be there, while ventilation is air moving through an opening that was. Seal the first, keep the second. If you tighten up the house and then notice windows streaming with condensation in the mornings, the answer is better controlled airflow, not putting the draughts back; ventilation without losing heat covers the options, from using extractor fans properly through to heat recovery units.

A leaky front door is the rare energy problem where the complete fix costs less than a takeaway and survives for years. Test the door on a windy day, buy only the parts the test points to, and fit the threshold seal first because the gap under the door is almost always the worst leak. With the perimeter, letterbox, keyhole and threshold all sorted for £20 to £40, you remove a constant drain on the hallway radiator and make the whole ground floor easier to keep warm.

Keep it in perspective on the way past, though. Draughtproofing is the cheap first rung of an insulation ladder, and the rungs above it are bigger: loft insulation typically saves several times more per year, and the rest of the draught-proofing jobs around windows, floors and chimneys add up to around £85 a year in a typical semi. Do the door this weekend, then work upwards.

faq.q: Does draughtproofing a front door really save money?
faq.a: Yes. The Energy Saving Trust puts draught-proofing windows and doors at around £30 a year for a typical gas-heated semi, and the front door is usually the worst single leak because it combines the biggest perimeter gap with a letterbox and keyhole. A £20 to £40 kit normally pays for itself within one to two winters, and the hallway feels warmer immediately.

faq.q: What is the best way to draughtproof a letterbox?
faq.a: Fit an internal letterbox brush plate on the inside face of the door, ideally one with two rows of brush plus a hinged flap. They cost £5 to £12 and fit with four screws in about ten minutes. Check post still passes through after fitting. An external cowl over the outside flap adds a second barrier if the door is very exposed.

faq.q: Should I cover the keyhole on my front door?
faq.a: If it is a traditional mortice keyhole, yes. A covered escutcheon, a plate with a small swinging disc over the opening, costs £2 to £5 and stops the keyhole acting as a wind tunnel through the door. You flick it aside with the key, so it has no effect on daily use. Doors with modern euro cylinders generally do not need one.

faq.q: Can I draughtproof a front door in a rented home?
faq.a: Mostly, yes. Self-adhesive perimeter strip is cheap and removable, and a fabric draught excluder at the threshold needs no fixing at all. Screw-on items like letterbox plates and door bottom seals are minor alterations, so check with the landlord first, but few object to a tidy job that makes the property warmer.

faq.q: Will sealing my front door cause damp or condensation?
faq.a: Not if you only seal the accidental gaps. Damp problems start when people also block deliberate ventilation such as trickle vents, wall vents and extractor fans, or the air vents serving an open-flued boiler or fire, which must never be covered. If condensation appears after draughtproofing, improve controlled ventilation rather than reopening the draughts.
